mission.title: Install and Configure Distribution on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Distribution project provides a toolkit to pack, ship, store, and deliver container content. Installing it allows you to set up a private container registry that adheres to the OCI Distribution Specification, which is essential for managing container images effectively.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the JFrog Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add jfrog https://charts.jfrog.io
```

steps.title: Update the Helm repository
steps.description:
Update your Helm repositories to ensure you have the latest charts:
```bash
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Distribution Helm chart
steps.description:
Install the Distribution chart into a new namespace called 'distribution':
```bash
helm install distribution jfrog/distribution --namespace distribution --create-namespace --version 102.36.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the pods are running correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace distribution
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the deployment to set resource limits:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment distribution --namespace distribution
``` 
Add the following under spec.containers:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Distribution release:
```bash
helm uninstall distribution --namespace distribution
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any custom resource definitions (CRDs) related to Distribution:
```bash
kubectl delete crd distributions.distribution.example.com
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace created for Distribution:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace distribution
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your current configuration and data. You can use the following command to export your current deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment distribution --namespace distribution -o yaml > distribution-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update your Helm repositories to get the latest charts:
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Distribution Helm chart
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the Distribution release to the latest version:
```bash
helm upgrade distribution jfrog/distribution --namespace distribution --version 102.36.2
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the pods are running correctly after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace distribution
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are stuck in the Pending state, check for insufficient resources in your cluster:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace distribution
``` 
Ensure your cluster has enough CPU and memory resources available.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace distribution
``` 
Investigate the logs for any application errors that might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot reach the service, check the service configuration:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace distribution
``` 
Ensure the service is correctly configured and the correct ports are exposed.

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Distribution pods running in the 'distribution' namespace. You can verify this by checking the pod status and ensuring that the service is reachable.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
